pattern: Splitting the capped array into small elements (kept) and capped-large elements (all equal to x), a single subset-sum table over the smalls grown as x rises answers every cap value, with the large elements contributing fixed multiples of x.
hints: When you cap at x, elements at most x are unchanged while everything larger becomes exactly x, so the larger ones only contribute multiples of x.
So the question for each x is: can a subset of the unchanged (small) elements reach k minus j*x for some count j of capped-large elements you include?

Maintain a boolean subset-sum table over values up to k for the small elements, and as x increases by one, fold in the elements equal to x; then test k, k-x, k-2x, ... against the table, bounded by how many large elements exist.

edge_inputs.description: single element smaller than k is never enough
large elements only help via multiples of x
all ones, only small sums reachable
k larger than any reachable sum for small caps

stress.description: random arrays with values bounded by n and a mid-range target
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    nums = [rng.randint(1, n) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, min(4000, n))
    return [nums, k]
stress.note: values capped at n per constraints; k kept within range
